title: Machine-Learning Prediction of Quantum Fisher Information from Collective Spin and Spectral Features
authors: Yusef Maleki, Luis D. Zambrano Palma
abstract: Quantum Fisher information (QFI) is a fundamental quantifier in quantum metrology, determining the ultimate precision achievable in parameter-estimation protocols through the quantum Cramér-Rao bound. However, direct evaluation of the QFI generally requires detailed knowledge of the density matrix, making it increasingly demanding as the Hilbert-space dimension grows. In this work, we investigate the extent to which the QFI of multipartite quantum systems can be predicted from a limited set of experimentally accessible quantities using support vector regression (SVR). By comparing different physically motivated features, we identify a dominant feature set governing QFI and show that the predictive power of collective spin moments alone decreases as system size and consequently Hilbert-space dimension grows. We demonstrate that QFI is governed primarily by the interplay between collective covariance and low-order spectral moments of the density matrix. Our results identify the physically relevant information sectors governing the QFI and demonstrate that accurate estimation of metrological sensitivity can be achieved from a restricted set of experimentally accessible quantities without requiring full quantum-state tomography.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), a key metric in quantum metrology, can be accurately predicted using machine learning (SVR) trained on low-order collective spin and spectral moments. The results show that while simple spin moments are insufficient, combining these with spectral quantities like purity significantly enhances predictive power, offering a pathway to estimate quantum sensitivity without full quantum state tomography.
main_problem: The core problem is predicting the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for multipartite quantum systems without performing computationally intensive full quantum-state tomography.
main_result: QFI can be accurately predicted using machine learning models trained on a reduced set of physically accessible quantities, specifically the interplay between collective covariance and low-order spectral moments.
method: Support Vector Regression (SVR) with an RBF kernel is employed to learn the nonlinear mapping between a set of low-order collective spin and spectral observables and the target QFI.
model_or_system: The study focuses on multipartite quantum systems, ranging from two to five qubits, analyzed using random pure, mixed, and hybrid quantum states.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), collective spin moments ($\langle\hat{J}_iangle$), second-order moments ($\langle\hat{J}_i^2angle$), symmetrized correlators ($\langle\hat{J}_i\hat{J}_j + \hat{J}_j\hat{J}_iangle$), Purity ($	ext{Tr}(ho^2)$), and the third spectral moment ($	ext{Tr}(ho^3)$).
important_parameters: System size (number of qubits, $N=2$ to $5$), and the specific structure of the quantum state (pure, mixed, hybrid).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the metrological sensitivity (QFI) is governed by a compact, low-dimensional set of experimentally measurable observables, bypassing the need for full state reconstruction.
figures_summary: Figures compare exact QFI values against SVR predictions using different feature sets (e.g., spin moments only vs. spin moments + purity + $	ext{Tr}(ho^3)$), showing increasing accuracy with the inclusion of higher-order spectral terms. One figure tracks performance degradation as the number of qubits increases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces QFI, details the SVR methodology, and then systematically tests the predictive power by comparing feature sets: first only spin moments, then adding purity ($	ext{Tr}(ho^2)$), and finally adding the cubic spectral moment ($	ext{Tr}(ho^3)$), analyzing results for both two-qubit and larger multipartite systems.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, data-driven methodology for characterizing metrological resources in complex quantum systems, suggesting practical ways to estimate quantum limits using limited experimental measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Comparison between exact and SVR-predicted quantum Fisher information using (a) linear, (b) polynomial (d = 2), and (c) RBF kernels. The dashed line denotes the ideal agreement between the exact and predicted QFI values.

FIG. 2. Prediction of the quantum Fisher information using differ- ent sets of collective observables within the RBF-SVR model. (a) First moments only, (b) second moments only, (c) symmetrized cor- relators only, and (d) second moments combined with symmetrized correlators. The dashed line denotes the ideal agreement between the exact and predicted QFI values.

FIG. 3. Comparison between the exact and SVR-predicted quan- tum Fisher information for external depolarized two-qubit Bell-state family as a function of the depolarizing parameter p. The solid and dashed curves represent the exact and SVR-predicted QFI values, re- spectively.

FIG. 4. Comparison between exact and SVR-predicted quantum Fisher information for multipartite systems composed of (a) two qubits, (b) three qubits, (c) four qubits, and (d) five qubits using collective spin moment features. The dashed line denotes the ideal agreement between the exact and predicted QFI values.

Figure 4 shows the dispersion plots between the predicted and exact QFI values for the two-, three-, four-, and five-qubit cases. A clear progressive degradation in the prediction ac- curacy is observed as the number of qubits increases, indicat- ing that the selected 15 features set [Eq. (14)] become pro- gressively less informative as the dimension of the Hilbert space grows. Specifically, in Fig. 4(a), corresponding to the two-qubit case, the predicted values remain strongly local- ized around the diagonal prediction line, while the three-qubit system shown in Fig. 4(b), noticeable deviations from the ideal prediction line begin to emerge.
